text: The products in the following free radical reaction are ('•' denotes a free radical): A trigonal planar free radical carbon (bearing \(\text{CH}_3\) and \(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\)) reacts with Cl• to give two products.
explanation:
This is the **chain termination step** of a free radical substitution. The carbon bearing the free radical is **\(sp^2\) hybridised** and is **trigonal planar** — an achiral intermediate.

When Cl• attacks this planar radical centre:
- 50% attack from front → one enantiomer (retention)
- 50% attack from back → the other enantiomer (inversion)

The result is an **equimolar mixture of d- and l-forms = racemic mixture**, which is optically inactive.
name: Questions Item 1

text: Geometrical isomerism can be found in which of the following?
explanation:
Geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism requires a **C=C double bond** where each sp² carbon bears **two different groups**.

- Butyric acid (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}\)): no C=C → no isomerism
- Aspartic acid: no relevant C=C
- Palmitic acid: saturated → no isomerism
- **Cinnamic acid** (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{—CH=CH—COOH}\)): the C=C has \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\)/H on one carbon and COOH/H on the other → **shows cis and trans isomers** ✓
name: Questions Item 2

text: Suitable conditions to prepare hexachlorobenzene from benzene are
explanation:
Hexachlorobenzene is formed by **free radical addition** of chlorine to benzene (adding across the ring double bonds, not substitution):

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV, 500 K}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_6\]

UV light initiates the free radical chain mechanism. \(\text{AlCl}_3\) is used for electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions — not for this addition reaction.
name: Questions Item 3

text: Which of the following products indicates the presence of sulphur atom in an organic compound in qualitative elemental analysis?
explanation:
In **Lassaigne's test** for sulphur detection:

1. Na fusion converts organic S → \(\text{Na}_2\text{S}\)
2. The aqueous extract is treated with **lead acetate**:

\[\text{Na}_2\text{S}(aq) + (\text{CH}_3\text{COO})_2\text{Pb} \rightarrow \textbf{PbS}\downarrow\text{(black)} + 2\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}\]

Formation of a **black precipitate of PbS** confirms sulphur.
name: Questions Item 4

text: Identify the primary halide among the following compounds.
explanation:
The degree of a halide depends on how many carbons are attached to the carbon bearing the halogen:

- **1-bromo-but-2-ene**: \(\overset{1°}{\text{CH}_2}(\text{Br})\text{—CH=CH—CH}_3\) → Br on a carbon bonded to only one other C → **primary halide** ✓
- 4-bromo-pent-2-ene: Br on a secondary C → 2°
- 2-bromo-2-methylpropane: \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CBr}\) → tertiary (3°)
- t-butyl bromide: tertiary (3°)
name: Questions Item 5

text:
Arrange the following carbocations in the increasing order of stability:

Label 1: \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CH}^+\),  Label 2: \(\text{CH}_3^+\),  Label 3: \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+\),  Label 4: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2^+\)
explanation:
Carbocation stability ∝ number of **hyperconjugative structures** ∝ number of α-H atoms:

| Label | Carbocation | α-H | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | \(\text{CH}_3^+\) | 0 | Least |
| 4 | \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2^+\) | 3 | 2nd |
| 1 | \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CH}^+\) | 6 | 3rd |
| 3 | \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+\) | 9 | Most |

Increasing order: **2 < 4 < 1 < 3**
name: Questions Item 6

text: Which of the following compounds will exhibit cis-trans isomerism?
explanation:
Cis-trans isomerism requires a **C=C** bond where **each sp² carbon has two different groups**:

- **2-butene** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH—CH}_3\)): both C have CH₃ and H → **cis and trans forms exist** ✓
- 2-butyne: triple bond is linear → no geometric isomerism
- 2-butanol: no C=C
- Butanal: no C=C
name: Questions Item 7

text: In an agricultural field, which among the following is the highest producer of methane?
explanation: **Paddy (rice) fields** are the largest agricultural source of methane. Rice is grown in **flooded, waterlogged fields** where anaerobic conditions prevail. **Methanogenic bacteria** decompose organic matter in the absence of oxygen and produce \(\text{CH}_4\). Most of this methane passes through the rice plant tissue and is released to the atmosphere.
name: Questions Item 8

text: For a compound with empirical formula \(\text{C}_7\text{H}_8\text{O}\), how many aromatic structures are possible?
explanation:
For \(\text{C}_7\text{H}_8\text{O}\), the 5 aromatic isomers are:

1. Benzyl alcohol (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\))
2. o-Cresol (2-methylphenol)
3. m-Cresol (3-methylphenol)
4. p-Cresol (4-methylphenol)
5. Anisole (methoxybenzene, \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OCH}_3\))

Total = **5 aromatic structures**.
name: Questions Item 9

text: Which of the following can show geometrical isomerism?
explanation:
For geometrical isomerism, each carbon of the double bond must have **two different groups**:

- **\(\text{CHCl=CHCl}\)**: Left C has H and Cl; Right C has H and Cl → both carbons have different groups → **cis** (Cl's on same side) and **trans** (Cl's on opposite sides) isomers exist ✓
- \(\text{CH}_2\text{=CCl}_2\): Left C has H, H → identical → no geometric isomerism
- \(\text{CCl}_2\text{=CHCl}\): Left C has Cl, Cl → identical → no geometric isomerism
- \(\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2\): both C have H, H → no isomerism
name: Questions Item 10

text: The common name of isopropyl benzene is
explanation: **Cumene** is the common name for isopropyl benzene. Its IUPAC name is (propan-2-yl)benzene and molecular formula is \(\text{C}_9\text{H}_{12}\). Cumene is industrially important as the raw material in the **cumene process** for producing phenol and acetone.
name: Questions Item 11

text: The IUPAC name for \(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH=CHCH}_2\text{COCH}_3\) is
explanation:
The **ketone (C=O)** has highest priority and determines the numbering direction. Numbering from the ketone end:

\[\underbrace{\text{CH}_3}_{1}\underbrace{\text{—C=O}}_{2}\underbrace{\text{—CH}_2}_{3}\underbrace{\text{—CH}}_{4}\underbrace{=\text{CH}}_{5}\underbrace{\text{—CH}_2}_{6}\underbrace{\text{—CH}_2}_{7}\text{Br}\]

- Ketone at C2 → suffix '-2-one'
- Double bond C4=C5 → 'hept-4-en'
- Br at C7 → '7-bromo'

Name: **7-bromohept-4-en-2-one**
name: Questions Item 12

text: Steam distillation process cannot be used for purifying which of the following?
explanation:
Steam distillation works for compounds that are **immiscible with water**, **steam volatile**, and **not decomposed by heat**.

**p-Nitrophenol** cannot be purified by steam distillation because it is **water-soluble** (forms hydrogen bonds with water). It cannot form a separate immiscible phase with water required for steam distillation.

Aniline, toluene, and nitrobenzene are water-immiscible and steam-volatile → they can be steam-distilled.
name: Questions Item 13

text: In which of the following reactions is an alkane NOT formed?
explanation:
- **(a) Wurtz reaction:** \(2\text{CH}_3\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\text{Na}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3\) → **ethane (alkane)** ✓
- **(b) Duma's decarboxylation:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{CaO}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\) → **methane** ✓
- **(c) Kolbe's electrolysis:** \(2\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 2\text{CO}_2\) → **ethane** ✓
- **(d) Dehydrohalogenation:** \(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\text{Alc. KOH}} \text{HC≡CH} + 2\text{HBr}\) → **acetylene (alkyne), NOT alkane** ✓
name: Questions Item 14

text: The number of isomers of \(\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}\) is
explanation:
\(\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}\) (pentane) has exactly **3 structural isomers**:

1. **n-Pentane**: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3\)
2. **Isopentane (2-methylbutane)**: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH(CH}_3)_2\)
3. **Neopentane (2,2-dimethylpropane)**: \(\text{C(CH}_3)_4\)

No geometric or optical isomers exist for pentane isomers. Total = **3**.
name: Questions Item 15

text: Sodium acetate was electrolysed by Kolbe's method forming two gases A and B at the anode. C and D are formed when B is heated with regulated \(\text{O}_2\) in presence of \((\text{CH}_3\text{COO})_2\text{Mn}\). C reacts with NaOH to form a salt. A and D are respectively
explanation:
Kolbe's electrolysis of \(\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+\) at anode:

\[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^\bullet \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CO}_2}_{\text{(B)}} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet\]
\[\text{CH}_3^\bullet + \text{CH}_3^\bullet \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}_{\text{(A)}}\]

B (\(\text{CO}_2\)) + \(\text{O}_2\) with catalyst forms:
\[2\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 7\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{CO}_2 + 6\underbrace{\text{H}_2\text{O}}_{\text{(D)}}\]
\[\underbrace{\text{CO}}_{\text{(C)}} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{HCOONa}\text{ (salt)}\]

∴ **A = \(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\)**, **D = \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)**
name: Questions Item 16

text:
The stability order of the following resonance structures is:

I. \(\text{CH}_2\text{=CH—CHO}\) (neutral, complete octet)
II. \(\overset{+}{\text{CH}}_2\text{—CH=C—}\overset{-}{\text{O}}\text{H}\) (positive on C, negative on O)
III. \(\overset{-}{\text{CH}}_2\text{—CH=C—}\overset{+}{\text{O}}\text{H}\) (negative on C, positive on O)
explanation:
**Structure I**: Neutral, complete octets → **most stable**

**Structure II**: Negative charge on **oxygen** (electronegative → prefers negative charge); positive on C → more stable than III

**Structure III**: Positive charge on **oxygen** (electronegative atoms should NOT carry positive charge) and negative on C → **least stable**

Stability order: **III < II < I**
name: Questions Item 17

text:
Which of the following statements about benzene reactions are correct?

I. In nitrating mixture, nitric acid participates as an acid.
II. σ-complex is the intermediate in electrophilic substitution of benzene.
III. Benzene on Friedel-Crafts alkylation with n-propyl chloride gives isopropyl benzene.
explanation:
**Statement I — Incorrect:** In the nitrating mixture (\(\text{HNO}_3\) + \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\)), \(\text{HNO}_3\) acts as a **base** (accepts a proton from \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\)) to generate the electrophile \(\text{NO}_2^+\).

**Statement II — Correct:** The **σ-complex** (arenium ion) is the intermediate in electrophilic aromatic substitution — one ring carbon becomes \(sp^3\) hybridised upon electrophile attack.

**Statement III — Correct:** n-propyl chloride generates 1° carbocation which rearranges to more stable 2° carbocation → **isopropyl benzene** (cumene) is the product.

Correct: **II and III**.
name: Questions Item 18

text: Which one of the following functional groups is NOT meta-directing?
explanation:
Meta-directing groups are **electron-withdrawing (EWG)** groups with a C=O or N=O bond:

- **—COOH**: C=O with electronegative O → meta-directing
- **—NO₂**: N=O with electronegative O → meta-directing
- **—CHO**: C=O with electronegative O → meta-directing
- **—OCH₃**: O has lone pairs with **+M effect** (donates electrons to ring, especially at ortho and para positions) → **ortho/para-directing**, NOT meta-directing ✓
name: Questions Item 19

text:
In the following resonance structures, the curved arrow indicates electrons are shifted from:

(A) \(\text{—O—CH}_2 \rightarrow \overset{+}{\text{—O}}\text{=CH}_2\)

(B) \(\text{C=N—} \rightarrow \text{C—}\overset{+}{\text{N}}\text{=}\)
options: Atom of adjacent bond in both (A) and (B)
π-bond to adjacent atom in both (A) and (B)
π-bond to adjacent atom in (A) and atom to adjacent bond in (B)
Atom to adjacent bond in (A) and π-bond to adjacent atom in (B)
explanation:
**In (A):** The lone pair electrons on the **O atom** move to form a new π-bond with adjacent C. This is **atom (lone pair) → adjacent bond**.

**In (B):** The **π-bond** electrons between C and N shift toward N (N is electronegative and pulls electrons). This is **π-bond → adjacent atom (N)**.

Correct: **(A) atom to adjacent bond; (B) π-bond to adjacent atom**.
name: Questions Item 20

text: In the detection of nitrogen in an organic compound by Lassaigne's test, Prussian blue colour is obtained due to the formation of which complex?
explanation:
In Lassaigne's test for nitrogen:
1. Na fusion: organic N → NaCN
2. \(6\text{CN}^- + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow [\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}\)
3. \(3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-} + 4\text{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \textbf{Fe}_4[\textbf{Fe(CN)}_6]_3 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}\)

**\(\text{Fe}_4[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]_3\)** is **Prussian blue** — iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II). Its formation confirms nitrogen in the organic compound.
name: Questions Item 21

text: The IUPAC name of a cyclohexene with —OCH₃ at position 4 is
explanation:
In IUPAC naming of alicyclic compounds:
- The **double bond** gets the **lowest locant** → C1=C2
- Numbering continues to give OCH₃ the next lowest number → C4

Name: **4-methoxycyclohexene**

(Note: Double bond is at C1=C2; methoxy group is at C4)
name: Questions Item 22

text:
Identify the ortho and para directing groups from the following:

I. CHO,  II. NHCOCH₃,  III. OCH₃,  IV. SO₃H
explanation:
- **CHO**: EWG with C=O → **meta-directing**
- **SO₃H**: EWG → **meta-directing**
- **NHCOCH₃ (acetamido)**: N has lone pairs, +M effect → donates electrons to ortho/para positions → **ortho/para-directing** ✓
- **OCH₃ (methoxy)**: O has lone pairs, +M effect → **ortho/para-directing** ✓

Correct: **II and III**.
name: Questions Item 23

text:
Assertion (A): \(S_N1\) hydrolysis of optically active 2-bromooctane results in the formation of (±)-octan-2-ol.

Reason (R): The reaction proceeds through a planar carbocation which can be attacked by the nucleophile from either side.
options: (A) and (R) are correct; (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) — Correct:** \(S_N1\) gives racemic product (±)-octan-2-ol.

**Reason (R) — Correct and explains A:**
1. In \(S_N1\), the C—Br bond breaks to form a **trigonal planar (sp²) carbocation** — an achiral intermediate
2. OH⁻ can attack from **either face** (front = retention, back = inversion) with equal probability
3. This 50:50 mixture is the **racemic mixture**

**Both A and R are correct, and R correctly explains A**.
name: Questions Item 24

text: Alkenes (X) and carbonyl compounds (Y) participate in which of the following addition reactions?
explanation:
**Alkenes (X) → Electrophilic addition:** The π-electron cloud of C=C is electron-rich and attracts **electrophiles** → alkenes undergo **electrophilic addition**.

**Carbonyl compounds (Y) → Nucleophilic addition:** The C=O bond is polarised \(\overset{\delta+}{\text{C}}\text{=}\overset{\delta-}{\text{O}}\). The carbon is electron-deficient → attacked by **nucleophiles** → carbonyl compounds undergo **nucleophilic addition**.
name: Questions Item 25

text:
The number of electrophiles and nucleophiles present in the following species are respectively:

\(\text{BF}_3\), \(\text{CO}_2\), \(\text{Me}_3\text{N}\), \(\text{SO}_3\), \(\text{CH}_3\text{CO}^+\), \(\text{HS}^-\), \(\text{NO}_2^+\), \(\text{FeCl}_3\), \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)
explanation:
**Electrophiles** (electron-deficient, accept lone pairs):
- \(\text{BF}_3\): incomplete octet on B ✓
- \(\text{CO}_2\): electrophilic C ✓
- \(\text{SO}_3\): electrophilic S ✓
- \(\text{CH}_3\text{CO}^+\): positive charge ✓
- \(\text{NO}_2^+\): cation ✓
- \(\text{FeCl}_3\): Lewis acid ✓

**Total electrophiles = 6**

**Nucleophiles** (electron-rich, donate lone pairs):
- \(\text{Me}_3\text{N}\): lone pair on N ✓
- \(\text{HS}^-\): negative, lone pairs on S ✓
- \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\): lone pairs on O ✓

**Total nucleophiles = 3**
name: Questions Item 26

text: The number of monochloro derivatives possible when 2,2-dimethylpropane reacts with chlorine in the presence of UV-light is
explanation:
2,2-Dimethylpropane (neopentane, \(\text{C(CH}_3)_4\)) has a central C with four identical methyl groups. All **12 hydrogen atoms are equivalent** (all are on primary carbons of identical methyl groups).

Replacing any one H by Cl gives the **same compound** — neopentyl chloride (\((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CCH}_2\text{Cl}\)).

Therefore, only **1 monochlorosubstitution product** is possible.
name: Questions Item 27

text:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

Benzene \(\xrightarrow{X}\) Nitrobenzene; Nitrobenzene \(\xrightarrow{Y}\) Benzenesulphonic acid
options: X = Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄/333 K; Y = H₂SO₄(SO₃)
explanation:
**Step 1 — Nitration (X):**
\[\text{Benzene} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. HNO}_3 + \text{Conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4,\;333\text{ K}} \text{Nitrobenzene}\]
\(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) protonates \(\text{HNO}_3\) to generate \(\text{NO}_2^+\) (nitronium ion electrophile).

**Step 2 — Sulphonation (Y):**
\[\text{Nitrobenzene} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(\text{SO}_3)} \text{Benzenesulphonic acid}\]
Fuming sulphuric acid/oleum provides \(\text{SO}_3\) as the electrophile.
name: Questions Item 28

text: Identify Z in the reaction: \(\text{ArX} + \text{RX} \xrightarrow{Z} \text{Ar—R}\) (Aryl halide + Alkyl halide → Arylalkane)
explanation:
The reaction where an **aryl halide** reacts with an **alkyl halide** using **sodium metal in dry ether** to form an arylalkane is the **Wurtz-Fittig reaction**:

\[\text{ArX} + \text{RX} + 2\text{Na} \xrightarrow{\text{Dry ether}} \text{Ar—R} + 2\text{NaX}\]

Z = **RX/Na/Dry ether**

(This differs from the simple Wurtz reaction which uses only alkyl halides.)
name: Questions Item 29

text: The IUPAC name of a compound containing a 6-carbon chain with a ketone (C=O) at C5 and a nitrile group (—CN) at C1 (CN has higher priority) is
explanation:
In IUPAC nomenclature, **nitrile (—CN)** has higher priority than a **ketone** as principal characteristic group. The CN carbon is C1:

\[\underbrace{\text{CN}}_{1}\text{—}\underbrace{\text{CH}_2}_{2}\text{—}\underbrace{\text{CH}_2}_{3}\text{—}\underbrace{\text{CH}_2}_{4}\text{—}\underbrace{\text{C=O}}_{5}\text{—}\underbrace{\text{CH}_3}_{6}\]

Name: **5-oxohexanenitrile** (6 carbons, keto at C5, nitrile suffix at C1).
name: Questions Item 30

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

(i) Petrol and CNG operated automobiles cause less pollution.
(ii) Alkanes having tertiary hydrogen can be oxidised to alcohols by \(\text{KMnO}_4\).
(iii) Methane can be prepared by Kolbe's electrolytic method.
(iv) Alkyl chloride on reduction with zinc and dilute hydrochloric acid gives alkane.
explanation:
**Statement (i) — Correct:** Petrol and CNG burn more cleanly than diesel, causing less particulate pollution.

**Statement (ii) — Correct:** \(\text{KMnO}_4\) selectively oxidises the tertiary C—H bond in alkanes to give tertiary alcohols.

**Statement (iii) — Incorrect:** Kolbe's electrolysis of **sodium acetate** gives **ethane** (not methane). Methane is prepared by decarboxylation of sodium acetate with NaOH/CaO.

**Statement (iv) — Correct:** \(\text{R—X} + \text{Zn} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{R—H} + \text{ZnXCl}\) (reduction gives alkane).

Correct: **(i), (ii), (iv)**.
name: Questions Item 31

text:
The order of priority of the following functional groups in IUPAC nomenclature is:

(i) \(\text{C=O}\) (ketone)  (ii) \(\text{NH}_2\)  (iii) CN  (iv) COOR (ester)
explanation:
IUPAC priority order of principal characteristic groups (highest → lowest):

\[\text{COOR (ester)} > \text{CN (nitrile)} > \text{C=O (ketone)} > \text{NH}_2\text{ (amine)}\]

Applied to the given groups: **(iv) COOR > (iii) CN > (i) C=O > (ii) NH₂**
name: Questions Item 32

text: Isopropyl benzene on aerial oxidation followed by acid hydrolysis of the resulting compound yields
explanation:
This is the **cumene process**:

**Step 1 — Aerial oxidation:**
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH(CH}_3)_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{C(CH}_3)_2\text{OOH}\]
(cumene hydroperoxide)

**Step 2 — Acid hydrolysis:**
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{C(CH}_3)_2\text{OOH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}}_{\text{phenol}} + \underbrace{(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO}}_{\text{acetone}}\]
name: Questions Item 33

text:
What are X, Y and Z in the following reactions?

\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COONa} \xrightarrow{X} \text{Y}\) (forward) and \(\xleftarrow{\text{Kolbe's Electrolysis}} \text{Z} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\) (reverse)
explanation:
**Forward (X → Y): Decarboxylation (Duma's method)**
\[\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COONa} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH+CaO},\Delta} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\]
X = **NaOH + CaO/Δ**, Y = **C₂H₆**

**Reverse (Kolbe's electrolysis produces Z):**
\[2\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \xrightarrow{\text{electrolysis}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3 + 2\text{CO}_2\]
Kolbe's of **sodium acetate** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}\)) produces ethane.
Z = **CH₃COONa** (sodium acetate)
name: Questions Item 34

text: Which one of the following compounds will NOT show geometrical isomerism?
explanation:
For geometrical isomerism, **each sp² carbon of C=C must have two different groups**.

- **Prop-2-enoic acid** (\(\text{CH}_2\text{=CH—COOH}\)): The terminal C (C1) has **H, H** — two identical groups → **no geometric isomerism** ✓
- 2-butene: \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH=CH—CH}_3\) → CH₃ and H on each C → cis/trans isomers exist
- 2-methyl-2-butenoic acid: CH₃ and COOH on adjacent C=C carbons → isomers exist
- 3-methyl-2-pentenoic acid: different groups on each C → isomers exist
name: Questions Item 35

text:
Match the following effects with their correct examples:

A. Resonance  B. Inductive effect  C. Electromeric effect  D. Hyperconjugation

I. \(\text{C=C} + \text{H} \rightarrow \text{C—C—H}\),  II. \(\text{H—CH}_2\text{—CH}_2\text{—H}\) (ethane),  III. \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_6\) (benzene),  IV. \(\text{CH}_3\text{—Z} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3^+ + \text{Z}^-\),  V. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)
explanation:
- **(A) Resonance → III (Benzene):** Classic example of electron delocalisation through resonance.
- **(B) Inductive effect → V (1-Chloropropane):** Cl withdraws electrons inductively along the C—C—C chain.
- **(C) Electromeric effect → I (C=C + H → C—C—H):** The electromeric effect involves complete transfer of π electrons under the influence of an attacking reagent (e.g., electrophilic addition to C=C).
- **(D) Hyperconjugation → II (Ethane):** Sigma C—H electrons interact with adjacent orbitals (hyperconjugation in carbocations, radicals, alkenes).
name: Questions Item 36
